# Ch 1 — Toolchain & Project Setup

> **Idea:** One tool (`uv`) replaces the entire Python environment mess; one mental model (`pathlib`) replaces every `os.path` call. Get these right and the rest of the book installs cleanly.

**Milestone:** `uv` venv created, Polars + Quarto installed, `hello.qmd` renders to HTML, `data/adam/*.parquet` populated.

---

## 1.1 Why the Python packaging story was painful (and isn't now)

If you've tried Python before and given up on the environment setup, you are not alone. The old story:

```
pip install polars          # installs globally, breaks other projects
python -m venv .venv        # creates a venv
source .venv/Scripts/activate  # activate (Windows is different from Mac)
pip install -r requirements.txt  # which may not pin transitive deps
pyenv install 3.11.8        # manage Python versions separately
```

Four tools, three mental models, platform-specific activation commands.

The new story is one tool.

---

## 1.2 `uv` — one tool to replace pip + venv + pyenv

`uv` is a Python package manager written in Rust. It is **10-100× faster** than pip and manages Python versions, virtual environments, and lockfiles in a single command.

### Install `uv` on Windows

```powershell
# In PowerShell (not Git Bash)
powershell -ExecutionPolicy ByPass -c "irm https://astral.sh/uv/install.ps1 | iex"
```

Restart your terminal, then verify:

```powershell
uv --version
# uv 0.4.x (or later)
```

### Install a specific Python version

```powershell
uv python install 3.11
uv python list          # see what's installed
```

### Bootstrap this book's project

```powershell
cd C:/Users/vt704670/repos/python_polars_book

# Create a venv pinned to Python 3.11
uv venv --python 3.11

# Install all book dependencies from pyproject.toml
uv sync --all-extras
```

That's it. No `activate`. Every `uv run <command>` automatically uses the local `.venv`.

```powershell
uv run python -c "import polars; print(polars.__version__)"
# 1.x.x
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## Pitfall: Forgetting `uv run`
If you type `python script.py` instead of `uv run python script.py` you'll use your system Python, not the venv. Alternatively, activate the venv manually once per session: `.venv\Scripts\activate` on Windows.
:::

---

## 1.3 Project layout — the structure we use throughout this book

```
python_polars_book/
├── _quarto.yml         ← Quarto book config
├── pyproject.toml      ← single source of truth for deps + package metadata
├── .python-version     ← "3.11" — tells uv which Python to use
├── index.qmd           ← preface
├── chapters/           ← one .qmd per chapter
├── data/
│   ├── adam/           ← synthetic CDISC pilot parquet files
│   └── definitive_guide/  ← Polars book example datasets
├── scripts/            ← one-time data fetch scripts
├── src/
│   └── clintools/      ← the library we build in Ch 6-8
├── tests/              ← pytest tests for clintools
└── docs/               ← mkdocs API documentation
```

This layout follows the `src/` convention — the package lives in `src/clintools/`, not in the root. The reason matters and is explained fully in Ch 6.

---

## 1.4 `pathlib` — the only way to handle paths

> **Rule:** Never write `os.path.join(...)`. Write `Path(...) / "subdir" / "file.csv"`.

```{python}
#| label: pathlib-intro
from pathlib import Path

# Walk up from CWD until we find _quarto.yml (= project root)
# Quarto 1.9 sets CWD to the chapter's folder, not the project root
def _find_root(p: Path) -> Path:
    for candidate in [p, *p.parents]:
        if (candidate / "_quarto.yml").exists():
            return candidate
    return p

PROJECT_ROOT = _find_root(Path.cwd())
DATA_DIR = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "adam"

# Build paths safely — works on Windows AND Linux (Domino)
adsl_path = DATA_DIR / "adsl.parquet"
print(adsl_path)          # C:\...\data\adam\adsl.parquet  (local)
                           # /mnt/.../data/adam/adsl.parquet  (Domino)

# Explore a directory
for f in DATA_DIR.glob("*.parquet"):
    print(f.name, f.stat().st_size)
```

Key `Path` methods you'll use constantly:

| Method | What it does |
|---|---|
| `Path.home()` | `C:/Users/vt704670` (Windows) or `/home/user` (Linux) |
| `path / "subdir"` | Join path components — never use `+` or `os.path.join` |
| `path.exists()` | True/False |
| `path.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)` | Create dir safely |
| `path.glob("*.parquet")` | Iterate matching files |
| `path.stem` | `"adsl"` from `"adsl.parquet"` |
| `path.suffix` | `".parquet"` |
| `path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")` | Read entire file as string |
| `path.write_text(content, encoding="utf-8")` | Write string to file |

::: {.callout-warning}
## Pitfall: Hardcoded Windows paths break on Domino
Never write `"C:/Users/vt704670/repos/..."` inside Python code that will run on Domino. Always build paths from `Path.home()`, `Path(__file__).parent`, or environment variables. Your path code should work identically on both platforms without change.
:::

---

## 1.5 Quarto — why `.qmd` files are better than plain `.ipynb`

A `.qmd` (Quarto Markdown) file is like a Jupyter notebook but stored as plain text. This means:

- `git diff` on a `.qmd` shows what actually changed (not JSON noise)
- You can edit it in any text editor
- `quarto render` converts it to HTML, PDF, or Word
- A whole folder of `.qmd` files becomes a book via `_quarto.yml`

### Install Quarto CLI (one time)

Download from [quarto.org/docs/get-started/](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/) — choose the Windows installer.

Verify:

```powershell
quarto --version
# 1.x.x
```

### Render the book

```powershell
cd C:/Users/vt704670/repos/python_polars_book
quarto render
```

Output goes to `_book/index.html`. Open it in a browser.

### Your first `.qmd` hello-world

Create `hello.qmd` in the book root:

````markdown
---
title: "Hello Polars"
format: html
---

```{python}
import polars as pl

df = pl.DataFrame({
    "subject": ["01-001", "01-002", "01-003"],
    "age": [65, 72, 58],
    "treatment": ["Drug A", "Placebo", "Drug A"],
})
df
```
````

Render it:

```powershell
quarto render hello.qmd
```

If you see an HTML table with 3 rows, your setup is working.

---

## 1.6 Fetching the book data (one-time)

Run the two fetch scripts once. They download and convert the synthetic CDISC pilot data and the *Definitive Guide* datasets:

```powershell
uv run python scripts/00_fetch_pilot_data.py
uv run python scripts/01_fetch_dg_data.py
```

Verify:

```{python}
#| label: verify-data
from pathlib import Path
import polars as pl

def _find_root(p):
    for c in [p, *p.parents]:
        if (c / "_quarto.yml").exists(): return c
    return p

adam_dir = _find_root(Path.cwd()) / "data" / "adam"
for f in sorted(adam_dir.glob("*.parquet")):
    df = pl.read_parquet(f)
    print(f"{f.name:20s}  {len(df):>5} rows  {len(df.columns):>3} cols")
```

---

## Recap

1. `uv` manages Python versions, venvs, and package locking — use `uv run <cmd>` to run in the venv.
2. `uv sync --all-extras` installs everything from `pyproject.toml` in one command.
3. Project layout: `src/` for the library, `chapters/` for the book, `data/` for datasets.
4. `pathlib.Path` is the only acceptable way to build file paths — never `os.path.join`, never hardcoded strings.
5. `.qmd` files are plain-text notebooks that `quarto render` converts to HTML; the whole book renders with a single `quarto render`.
